title: Edge functions log full Supabase error objects (may include Postgres internals)
evidence: console.error('submit_action: rpc error', error) — passes the full Supabase error object. The @supabase/supabase-js error shape includes message, code (Postgres error code), details, hint, and status. While these do not contain service-role keys, they can expose internal Postgres error codes, table/column names, constraint names, and query details to Supabase's structured log storage. The one exception (_shared/mod.ts:91) already does the right thing: console.error('rate_limit rpc error:', error.message).
impact: Postgres internal schema details (column names, constraint names, query fragments) visible in Supabase edge function logs, accessible to anyone with Supabase dashboard access. Not a secret exposure per se but provides enumeration assistance if logs are accessible to a broader audience.
recommendation: Follow the pattern in _shared/mod.ts:91 and log error.message (or error.code + error.message) rather than the full error object. E.g. console.error('submit_action: rpc error', error?.message ?? error?.code).
acceptance_criteria: All console.error calls in edge functions log error.message (not the full object)
Rate-limit log in _shared/mod.ts:91 already correct — no change needed there

title: generateCode() has modulo bias and small 36^4 ≈ 1.7M room-code space
evidence: generateCode() draws 4 bytes from crypto.getRandomValues(new Uint8Array(4)) and maps each byte via chars[b % 36]. Since 256 mod 36 = 4, values 0–3 (→ A,B,C,D) appear with probability 8/256 = 3.125% vs. the ideal 1/36 = 2.778% — a 12.5% relative overweight. Separately, the entire code space is 36^4 = 1,679,616 codes. With join_room rate-limited to 20/min per IP (enforced) and a room code collision retry (max 5 attempts) guarding uniqueness, brute-forcing a single private room from one IP requires on average 116,397 attempts for 10 active rooms or 1.16M for 1 room. Time to enumerate entire space from one IP ≈ 58 days; distributed across many IPs, practical.
impact: (1) Bias is cosmetic for this use case — the overweight on A/B/C/D is negligible and not security-relevant at these scales. (2) The small code space is the more material risk: for a private room, an attacker can eventually brute-force the code if rate limits are bypassed (e.g., rotating IPs) or if many rooms are open simultaneously. A malicious joiner who discovers a private room code can observe the lobby (player names/colors) even before the game starts. Severity is low because (a) rooms are ephemeral and short-lived, (b) the game has no PII or financial stakes, and (c) the current rate limit adds meaningful friction.
recommendation: (1) Increase code length to 6 chars (36^6 ≈ 2.18B) to make enumeration infeasible even without rate limits. (2) Optionally fix modulo bias by rejection-sampling (discard bytes ≥ 252, keeping the 252/256 prefix that divides evenly by 36). (3) Add a per-room failed-join counter and lock the room after N consecutive bad-code attempts.
acceptance_criteria: Room code space ≥ 10^9 OR a per-room failed-attempt lockout is implemented
generateCode passes a chi-squared bias test (optional but clean)

title: supabase/functions/.env.example is not explicitly gitignored (covered only by global .env catch-all)
evidence: .gitignore line 8 is a bare '.env' pattern, which git interprets as matching any file named '.env' anywhere in the tree (git check-ignore confirms: supabase/functions/.env IS ignored by this rule). However, .env.example files are intentionally not ignored and are committed. The .env.example documents SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY. If a developer creates a live supabase/functions/.env.local (a common Deno/Supabase CLI pattern) that variant is also covered by the global .env catch-all, but .env.local is not explicitly called out. Verified: no actual .env or .env.local file exists under supabase/functions/ today.
impact: Low: the current state is clean (no real key committed). The risk is that a developer adding supabase/functions/.env.local would not see it explicitly called out in .gitignore, and might not realize it is covered, leading to a check-in without noticing.
recommendation: Add explicit entries to .gitignore: 'supabase/functions/.env' and 'supabase/functions/.env.local'. Belt-and-suspenders is better than relying on a catch-all for files containing the service-role key.

title: nginx.conf and netlify.toml ship no HTTP security headers
evidence: nginx.conf defines a simple static-file server with no add_header directives. netlify.toml has no [[headers]] block. Neither config ships X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options, Content-Security-Policy, Referrer-Policy, or Permissions-Policy. The Canvas SPA loads JavaScript, makes cross-origin requests to Supabase, and renders user-supplied player names as DOM text nodes — a missing CSP is the main gap.
impact: Without X-Frame-Options or CSP frame-ancestors, the game can be embedded in a hostile iframe (clickjacking). Without X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff, MIME-sniffing attacks are possible on any served asset. Without a CSP, if an XSS were found (separate lens), the attacker has no browser-enforced restriction on exfiltration. Low severity because (a) no PII or auth tokens are present, (b) no XSS has been confirmed by this lens.
recommendation: Add to nginx.conf inside the server block: add_header X-Content-Type-Options nosniff; add_header X-Frame-Options DENY; add_header Referrer-Policy strict-origin-when-cross-origin; add_header Permissions-Policy interest-cohort=(). Add a [[headers]] block in netlify.toml with the same. A restrictive CSP (default-src 'self'; connect-src *.supabase.co; ...) is worthwhile but requires knowing all Supabase endpoints.
acceptance_criteria: nginx.conf adds at minimum X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options, Referrer-Policy
netlify.toml adds a [[headers]] block with at least the same three headers

title: @supabase/supabase-js client semver range (^2.45.0) allows major-minor drift beyond pinned Edge Function version (2.107.0)
evidence: client/package.json declares '@supabase/supabase-js': '^2.45.0'; package-lock.json resolves to 2.107.0. The Edge Function imports a pinned 'https://esm.sh/@supabase/supabase-js@2.107.0'. These are currently the same resolved version, but npm's ^ range allows any 2.x release. Dependabot will bump the npm version; the esm.sh pin must be updated manually. If they diverge, wire-format differences could emerge (though supabase-js is typically backward-compatible within major version).
impact: Low: same major version, stable API surface. Primary risk is operational (client/server API mismatch) rather than direct security impact. No known CVE in 2.45.0–2.107.0 range for supabase-js at knowledge cutoff.
recommendation: Pin the client package.json to '2.107.0' (exact) or add a comment requiring the esm.sh pin to be updated in lockstep whenever Dependabot bumps it. Alternatively, document the expected manual sync step in CLAUDE.md or a CONTRIBUTING note.
acceptance_criteria: client/package.json and _shared/mod.ts reference the same @supabase/supabase-js version, OR a process comment documents the sync requirement

title: No deno.lock file committed — esm.sh imports re-resolve on each deploy
evidence: The edge functions import from 'https://esm.sh/@supabase/supabase-js@2.107.0'. The .gitignore explicitly excludes deno.lock ('# Generated by deno check of the edge functions (local typecheck only). deno.lock'). The version is pinned by the @2.107.0 tag, which prevents version drift, but the integrity hash of the resolved module is NOT locked. If esm.sh were compromised or the content behind the version tag mutated, no lockfile would detect the change on the next deploy cache flush.
impact: Low: esm.sh with an exact version tag is low-risk for mutation, and Supabase's edge function deployment likely caches modules. However, supply-chain integrity for CDN-sourced modules is an industry-recognized risk (see Socket.dev, Snyk advisories on CDN typosquatting).
recommendation: Commit a deno.lock that captures the integrity hash of the esm.sh module, and update the .gitignore comment to clarify when re-locking is appropriate. Alternatively, vendor the supabase-js module into the repo under supabase/functions/_vendor/.
acceptance_criteria: A deno.lock with module integrity hashes is committed and kept up to date
OR the import is vendored locally
